original_abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) like OpenAI Codex are transforming the landscape of software development by generating code from natural language prompts. However, their integration into educational settings raises critical questions regarding student reliance and the potential for hindered learning. This paper presents a controlled study involving 69 novice programmers (ages 10-17) to evaluate the impact of AI code generators on introductory programming education. Participants completed a series of 45 Python authoring tasks followed by code-modification exercises; the experimental group was granted access to Codex, while the baseline group worked manually.

Our findings indicate that AI assistance significantly improved performance on original code-authoring tasks, yielding a 1.15x higher completion rate and 1.8x higher correctness scores. Crucially, access to Codex during training did not undermine learners' ability to manually modify code, where both groups performed comparably. Evaluation via immediate and one-week retention post-tests—conducted without AI access—showed no significant performance gap between the two groups; notably, however, students in the Codex group with higher prior computational thinking skills demonstrated significantly better retention. These results suggest that AI code generators can serve as effective scaffolding tools for novices, enhancing productivity and confidence without necessarily impeding the acquisition of fundamental programming concepts.
humanized_abstract:
Summary Abstract Using a Large Language Model (LLM) such as OpenAI Codex to generate code from a description in free text language is changing the way software is developed. Using these tools in classrooms raises a number of important pedagogical issues, for example about the workload on a student and potential learning barriers. This paper presents results of a controlled study of the effect of an aid for programming such as an AI code generator on students learning to program. The study involved 69 young novice programmers between the ages of 10 and 17. The participants worked on a total of 45 Python authoring tasks as well as code modification tasks. The experimental group could use the Codex tool to help them with the tasks while the control group had to do all the work manually.

Using this framework, our findings demonstrate that the deployment of an AI code generator can significantly increase novice programmers’ productivity in writing the original code, resulting in 1.15x more completed assignments and 1.8x more correct lines of code for coding assignments compared to manual coding. It is important to note that training on code provided by Codex did not affect students’ ability to later manually modify the code; both sets of students modified the provided code at a similar rate. Retrospective coding tests (both immediate-retrospective and one-week-retrospective) administered without access to Codex revealed that the manual and AI-assisted groups achieved comparable levels of code quality; interestingly, students who had access to Codex, and who scored higher on the computational thinking subset of the CT-QF had higher code quality one-week-retrospectively.

The experimental group could use the Codex tool to help them with the tasks while the control group had to do all the work manually.

Using this framework, our findings demonstrate that the deployment of an AI code generator can significantly increase novice programmers’ productivity in writing the original code, resulting in 1.15x more completed assignments and 1.8x more correct lines of code for coding assignments compared to manual coding. document.input:
Large Language Models (LLMs) like OpenAI Codex are transforming the landscape of software development by generating code from natural language prompts. However, their integration into educational settings raises critical questions regarding student reliance and the potential for hindered learning. This paper presents a controlled study involving 69 novice programmers (ages 10-17) to evaluate the impact of AI code generators on introductory programming education. Participants completed a series of 45 Python authoring tasks followed by code-modification exercises; the experimental group was granted access to Codex, while the baseline group worked manually.

Our findings indicate that AI assistance significantly improved performance on original code-authoring tasks, yielding a 1.15x higher completion rate and 1.8x higher correctness scores. Evaluation via immediate and one-week retention post-tests—conducted without AI access—showed no significant performance gap between the two groups; notably, however, students in the Codex group with higher prior computational thinking skills demonstrated significantly better retention. These results suggest that AI code generators can serve as effective scaffolding tools for novices, enhancing productivity and confidence without necessarily impeding the acquisition of fundamental programming concepts.
